Health Solutions
CHELEXAB I O S C I E N C E S
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE BIG IDEA IN 187 WORDS:
Think for a moment about what happens when you get a wound:
you expose the inside of your body to the outside world. What happens next?
Germs, bacteria, and other microbes flood in.
And this is always bad news.
Because once an infection happens your chances getting very sick get worse minute by minute. Your body
will start the fight against infection, and your doctor will pitch in too (more about this later.)
But there’s one issue that could determine 80% of your success - is the infection within a base of biofilm?
Unfortunately named, biofilm is not actually good for you. It is the foundation for 80% of human wound infections.
If you could address the biofilm problem, you would have a major opportunity to lower healthcare costs in the US,
and even save lives.
This deck is dedicated to the economic upside from solving the biofilm problem.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 66 --

PROJECT JUPITER
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 66 --

THE BIG IDEA IN 96 WORDS
If you’re a top 50 wireless carrier,
just two things keep you up at night:
This deck is dedicated to a technical solution for what is knows as the Interior Signal Problem.
First, the amount of bandwidth consumers are using. They seem to have an
insatiable desire for wireless bandwidth (and they want it cheap.)
Second, nearly every house, apartment and office has zones of signal
disruption: areas if poor interior coverage. In these disruption zones, your
signal drops to 1-bar and there’s almost nothing you can do about it.
Of these two problems, the bandwidth problem is well understood. Want to
solve it? Invest in infrastructure. Operate effeciently. Compete on price. That
was easy - problem solved.
Want to solve the interior signal problem? Not so easy.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 66 --

THE OPPORTUNITY IN 103 WORDS
To save humanity, you have to tackle some really nasty problems. You
already know about them: climate change, clean energy, and species loss.
To us, the problem of species loss stands out. Why?
Because it’s irreversible.
Areas where there is high species concentration have become exceedingly rare and
valuable —out of 192 countries, only 17 make the watch list. None are in Europe. The
U.S. is one. The remaining 15 are in South America, Central America, Africa, and Asia.
All are at risk. But many are difficult to reach. Others are dangerous to operate in
once you reach them. Some are prohibitively expensive to deal with. Several are
targeted by other organizations.
But, there are 4 countries with areas of biodiversity wealth and species
concentration that we have identified as reachable, affordable and saveable. Our
immediate focus is to defend these areas.
This prospectus describes one of our priorities, how we selected it, why it must be
defended, and what it costs to do so.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 66 --

In recent years, the United States
has added more wind energy to its
grid than any other country, with
a growth in power capacity of 45
percent to 16.8 GW in 2007.
WIND
oren@cabotenergy.com 2
By Oren D. Klaff
IN THE U.S., CLEAN ENERGY IS A MASSIVE MARKET. By
2013, it’s projected to be $92 BILLION. That means,
in just 3 years, about 35 percent of the electricity in
the national power grid will be from solar, wind or
biomass projects.
NOT ONLY IS THE MARKET LARGE—IT’S GROWING FAST.
As a result, developers are rushing to build ever-
larger wind/solar/biomass projects, where they can
generate big profits. To raise capital, they’re selling
off their existing, smaller projects. This is where we
enter the market. We buy those projects as they
come up for sale, because they provide stable,
reliable, and secure cash flows.
This book explains how we find, analyze, and acquire these
clean energy assets.
CLEAN ENERGY

THE BIG IDEA IN 139 WORDS
This summary is a discussion of Bryan Brothers Athletics performance apparel business.
The sports apparel market is changing.
Today, new brands are ringing up huge retail
sales by rapidly expanding from sports niches
into mainstream retail.
IN YOGA, IT WAS LULULEMON ATHLETICA, a $10M microbrand that became a $2B
economic juggernaut. In football, it was Under Armour. In hiking, it was
The North Face, and in snowboarding it was Burton.
Now a niche tennis brand is emerging in this same pattern.
Bryan Brothers Athletics is an iconic apparel line for tennis players who aren’t fully
satisfied with today’s mass-produced tennis gear. The brand is for fans and players
who want through-the-day fashion, and who are inspired by Bob and Mike Bryan’s
winning style. And of course, it’s for those who are inspired by the internationally
known “chest bump” — which you will learn more about in a moment.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 66 --

THE BIG IDEA IN 143 WORDS:
If you wanted to describe western North Dakota in just a few words, you might say,
Fast Growing Economy. Or Beautiful Country. Or Nice People.
But if you really wanted to capture what is going on, you could simply say, NO VACANCY.
There’s limited housing supply in this fast growing region. Why? The growth in the petroleum business is one
of the key drivers. But the credit crunch is a culprit too. And there isn’t enough power, water and emergency
infrastructure to support the needed housing growth. Even if there were infrastructure, communities don’t
want outsiders to build “throw-away” housing - so permits are hard to get.
The result of all this? If you owned high qualilty multifamily units in this market, you’d enjoy 100%
occupancy, rising rents, limited competition and high net profits.
This book is dedicated to describing the assets we have for sale in western North Dakota.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 66 --

THE BIG IDEA IN 40 WORDS:
There’s nothing worse than discovering that you are trading against the market
-- especially against a major movement. But what if you had a team of the
world’s best rate forecasters confi rming your portfolio decisions each month?
That would be a real advantage.
LAB is a 30-day interest rate outlook that forecasts the direction and magnitude of change in
long-term interest rates, as well as the spread between long- and short-term interest rates. The
LAB signals give you support entry, hold and exit decisions and, as an overlay trade, can add 30 to
50 basis points without changing risk limits. Over time, LAB can keep you on the right side of the
market and improve timing decisions.
Select Signals from the Lab Model Prior to Event
SIGNALS FROM THE LAB MODEL FORECAST: REVERSALS

of the market turn.

THE BIG IDEA IN 119 WORDS
The US Angel Market provides most of the seed-stage funding in the United States. It has
an estimated long-term annual return over 27%.
But there’s a problem in the market. Th e way most angel investors evaluate deals tends to
screen out the huge home runs – what are known as “Black Swans.” And in this market,
Black Swans are what drive returns.
We take a dramatically different approach to the market. Instead of trying to out-pick
the market, we invest in enough companies to ensure that we get our fair share of
Black Swans. And we do it with a screen that tends to capture Black Swans rather than
screening them out. Th is book is dedicated to explaining how.
www.getwsodownload.com

THE BIG IDEA IN 96 WORDS
VOXOX is our unified communications app. We have registered approximately 500,000 users.
And unlike other communication apps, we have our own carrier-grade hardware—
and we plug directly into the phone company’s hardline PSTN.
This is not only a technical advantage, it’s a serious competitive advantage.
Having our own hardware provides us gross margins of 50-80% on voice and
SMS transmission. That matters. Why?
Because our competitors — who must buy these services from others —
have gross margins of 5% or less.
With this advantage, VOXOX projects 136,000 paid users and
$20M in proforma revenues in 2012.
This book is dedicated to the VOXOX opportunity.

Th is book is dedicated to explaining how these assets are managed and liquidated by
Nexxica and the company’s approach to underwriting.
A $1.22M portfolio of fi rst-position mortgage notes with
current cash fl ow, equity protection and reserves
NEXXICA SERIES 3 is a portfolio of 9 first-position residential mortgage notes
generating a current 12.3% cash-on-cash yield.
Th ese 9 notes were acquired from a money center bank as part of a larger trade, at a
discount of approximately 30% to current market value.
Nexxica is micromanaging the portfolio to maximize cash flow and to refinance -or
liquidate- all assets for their collateral value during a three year hold.
The budget provides over $111K for reserves and servicing - set aside to protect the
collateral value of the portfolio. There is no management fee. The Investor receives all
interest income during the hold period and the Investor will recapture 100% of equity
invested before profit participation by the Manager.

THE OPPORTUNITY IN 134 WORDS
This summary explains how the Manager of the Fund anticipates underwriting,
acquiring and managing the assets of the Fund.
A $300M Fund of real estate ground leases with steady cash fl ow from
high-quality tenants, providing security and income growth.
As investors peer into 2011, financial markets seem to be improving – but they
aren’t doing so on their own. Policy makers are using strong capital controls to push
markets higher, manufacturing a second year of recovery from the financial crises.
At some point, likely soon, the government will likely stop stimulus in the market
segments they think are healthy. As stimuli stop (and sometimes start again) there
may be unpredictable movement and volatility in the broader market.
For investors seeking to avoid such volatility trades, the real estate ground lease can
be a relatively secure position in the real estate market.
Unlike equities, commodities or other real estate assets, the ground lease can provide
tremendous security for investors - and a long term, stable source of revenue.

THE OPPORTUNITY IN HEATHCARE:
When it comes to delivering cash flow,
it’s not hard to understand the advantages of Healthcare over other industries:
high margins and steady demand. 	And it’s one-fifth of the American economy.
For these reasons, some healthcare investors will search for a “prize deal”- a single investment
the market has somehow overlooked. They want an easy win.
Try to outsmart the market by finding one perfect deal? That’s not us.
We are pragmatists. We know the risks of overreaching. So, while it may not be glamorous or have
razzledazzle, the Strategic Healthcare Fund anticipates it can provide what a single deal cannot:
secure cash flow in the first 12 months and attractive upside in approximately 36 months.

-- 28 of 66 --

3	page
22 Million Passengers are Projected by 2020
In the next decade the City of San Diego may have too many
commercial flights -and not enough runway
This is one of the busiest and most complex
airspace regions in the U.S.
By Oren Klaff and Richard Sax
San Diego International Airport (SDIA) is the second busiest single-runway airport in the
world, behind London Gatwick. With more than 22 million passengers projected to emplane
at SDIA in 2020, it’s nearing capacity. This is increasing the demand for runway space (and
aviation services) in the City of San Diego.
As demand increases, private, charter and corporate aircraft are facing higher costs and
tighter scheduling at SDIA. Ground services and parking availability are also affected.
General aviation aircraft operators need alternatives, but they can’t all go north to McClellan-
Palomar Airport — at 33 miles away, that runway is far from San Diego’s Central Business
District. One solution is our project: the Metropolitan Airpark at Brown Field Airport.
Metropolitan Airpark is well located along San Diego’s main commercial routes. The FAA
considers the runway at Brown Field a vital link in the nation’s aviation network. It is a key
point of entry to the City of San Diego and plays an important role relieving aircraft traffic
at SDIA.
This book is dedicated to introducing the Metropolitan Airpark and the opportunity to invest
in its growth and development.
THE OPPORTUNITY IN 185 WORDS
SAN DIEGO METROPOLITAN AIRPARK
San Diego needs additional general aviation services and amenities, on an adequate
runway, in a central location. Here’s why:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE BIG IDEA IN 187 WORDS:
Think for a moment about what happens when you get a wound:
you expose the inside of your body to the outside world. What happens next?
Germs, bacteria, and other microbes flood in.
And this is always bad news.
Because once an infection happens your chances of getting very sick get worse minute by minute. Your body
will start the fight against the infection, and your doctor will pitch in too (more about this later).
But there’s one issue that could determine 80% of your success—is the infection within a base of biofilm?
Unfortunately named, biofilm is not actually good for you. It is the foundation for 80% of human infectious diseases.
If you could address the biofilm problem, you would have a major opportunity to lower healthcare costs in the US,
and even save lives.
This deck is dedicated to the economic upside from solving the biofilm problem.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
These bacteria begin to form biofilm almost immediately. When the biofilm is
fully formed, the bacteria become resistant to treatments like debridement
and up to 1,000 times more resistant to antimicrobials.
This is not a rare problem, and it’s not cheap. It doesn’t occur in just the old,
the poor, or those in ill health.
Treating infected wounds is also quite expensive.
WHEN YOU GET A CUT OR ABRASION, a battle rages at the microbial level between your
body’s defenses and bacteria in the wound.
ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE DRAMATICALLY
DISRUPTS HEALTHCARE IN THE US
There are an average 1.7 million hospital acquired infections reported
annually at a cost of $45 billion.

ANNUAL

AQUIRED
INFECTIONS
!HAI’S"
= $ 45

-- 35 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
Most people know about products like Neosporin, alcohol, and gauze. As simple as that sounds, those
elements represent the main methods hospitals use as well. And if you get a minor wound, these options
are likely to work. But if you get surgery, or have a chronic wound (such as a diabetic foot ulcer or pressure
ulcer), or suffer a more traumatic wound, infection can put you at serious risk. Bacteria compete with
growing healthy cells for nutrients and interfere with the healing process. Hospitals use three methods to
fight infection in these cases.
However, none of these treatment protocols address the problem of biofilm, which is what makes infections
so difficult to treat in the first place.
HOSPITALS AND URGENT CARE FACILITIES RELY on three methods to handle infection. If any
of these treatments don’t work, your chances of getting very sick go up substantially.
THE 4TH LEADING CAUSE OF DEATH
And there are only three treatment options
70%
of the bacteria
causing HAI’s
are resistant to at
least one drug being
used to treat the
condition.
1. Remove It.
In technical medical terms
this is called
debridement. It is
the removal—that is surgically
cutting out—of a patient’s dead,
damaged, or infected tissue.
2. Kill It.
This is the use of antimicrobials
and includes antibiotics applied
topically (creams or foams) or
given intravenously.
3. Cover it.
Dressings are used in an attempt
to seal out bacteria and control
moisture content. Many come
with additives to absorb excess
moisture or assist in healing.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
Antimicrobial resistance “is a problem at
the national level and at the local level.
It’s a problem for patients, it’s a problem
for clinicians,...and it’s a problem for
other health officials.
James Hughes, MD, director of the program for global infectious diseases at the
Emory University School of Medicine in Atlanta, Georgia, and president of the IDSA
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 37 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
The science behind this product is long and complex,
and for those with a science inclination the sidebar
at the right goes into more detail on how Biofilm
is formed and how BioLexa prevents it. But in the
simplest terms, research is being done in wound care
every day, and recent research discovered something
interesting about biofilm.
In order to form, biofilm needs a commonplace
mineral—zinc.
Zinc enables intercellular adhesion of the bacteria.
Remove the zinc, and the bacteria cannot build their
protective biofilm because the cellular building blocks
won’t bind together.
Without a protective mechanism, bacteria are much
more susceptible to antibiotics.
It turns out a salt approved for human use since the
1960s called
Diethylenetriaminepentaacetate (DTPA)
removes, or chelates, the zinc—thus preventing
biofilm formation. DTPA is a small percentage of the
BioLexa cream.
When combined with a broad spectrum antibiotic, our
product prevents the formation of a biofilm so that
antibiotics can kill the bacteria.
ERADICATE BIOFILM, STOP INFECTION
The Chelexa product BioLexa eliminates biofilm and cures infection
BIOFILM IS THE REASON THAT THE TRADITIONAL TREATMENT PROTOCOLS of debridement and antibiotics are
costly, painful, and in some cases, completely ineffective.
HOW BIOLEXA WORKS
In order to form, biofilm needs a
commonplace mineral–zinc. Zinc
enables intercelluar adhesion of
the bacteria.
Without the zinc, the bactera
cannot build their protective
biofilm. BioLexa’s cream contains
DTPA which chelates zinc.
Source: Intersection Capital
Source: Intersection Capital
A biofilm is an aggregate of microorganisms in which cells adhere to each
other on a surface. These adherent cells are frequently embedded within
a self-produced matrix of extracellular polymeric substance (EPS). Biofilm
EPS, which is also referred to as slime (although not everything described
as slime is a biofilm), is a polymeric conglomeration generally composed of
extracellular DNA, proteins, and polysaccharides.
Planktonic
Induced to synthesize biofilm
Antibiotic-tolerant
ENVIRONMENTAL
STRESS
arr
PYSIOLOGICAL
DIFFERENTIATION
ndvB
ADHESION 	BIOFILM FORMATION 	PROTECTION
Zn2+
AA/SASG!MEDIATED ZINC ZIPPER
ZINC MEDIATED G5
DOMAIN DIMERIZATION
WHAT IS BIOFILM
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 38 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
Below are the four most important issues facing an investor in a wound care technology and
company. Here’s how we would screen a deal:
PROVEN EFFICACY:
First, you want proof that a treatment or product works as advertised. A recent example is the
introduction of silver in wound dressings in 2002 to both kill bacteria and speed healing. Since
introduction, no research has proven that silver dressings provide any healing benefit. The 2010
VULCAN trial concluded that there was no evidence to support the routine use of silver dressings.
Today silver dressings cost healthcare providers over $400 million per year, although investors in
silver dressings may see increased risk as product credibility erodes.
CLEAR REIMBURSEMENT PATHWAY:
A critical screen to keep in mind: Is there a clear and existing reimbursement pathway through
the centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services for healthcare providers and physicians? If such
a reimbursement pathway is not clear, providers will be reluctant to prescribe the product—even
if it works. In 2011 Dendreon Corp stock fell 67% when sales growth of its prostate cancer drug,
Provenge, slowed dramatically due to reimbursement difficulties that were delaying Provenge’s
adoption at smaller physician practices.
SHORT, ACHIEVABLE FDA APPROVAL:
Is the FDA approval pathway clear and possible? Many products look promising, but then cannot
get through the FDA approval process and cease attempts during Phase III clinical trials - losing
millions of investor dollars along the way.
INEXPENSIVE TO USE:
How much does the product cost to use and administer for the healthcare institution? In wound
care, costs range from $150 per day for some treatments up to $1200 and $1800 per treatment for
others. If the infection is categorized by CMS as a “never-pay-event,” healthcare providers absorb
the cost of treating the patients using these expensive products.
INVESTING IN WOUND CARE: A PRIMER
What we’ve learned from successes and failures in the past 20 years
Not all wound care solutions become economically viable to offer appealing returns to investors.
Sure, the market for treatment is large ($20 billion in costs for healthcare providers and $4.6 billion
in revenue for product providers), but as in any technology market, there are do’s and don’ts:
In many ways, we are investors in
this market. We have both capital and
time invested in developing the Chelexa
technology, and have well developed
opinions on what works and what doesn’t:
In the would dressings sector, there are
established market leaders and mature,
stable products, with limited upside
opportunity and low return on capital.
We rate this sector and product category
as: avoid.
A quick analysis of the antimicrobials
product sector shows these products have
a very lengthy and costly FDA approval
process which can take years and millions
of dollars. We rate this sector and product
category as: avoid.
Companies and Products to Avoid
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 39 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
WOUND CARE TECHNOLOGIES IN 2012
Today there are four main approaches. Of these, only one is a new development.
DEBRIDEMENT
The “remove it” portion of the standard protocol. Surgery is used most often but is expensive
and painful. Another more recent and less painful method is a process known as Enzymatic
Debridement. Currently there is only one FDA approved enzymatic debridement product.
ANTIMICROBIALS
Antimicrobials—to include antibiotics—fall under the second step or “kill it” portion of the
standard protocol. Newer antibiotics continue to be researched and approved for use—despite
a cost-prohibitive FDA approval process. Why must new antibiotics continue to be developed?
Antibiotic resistant bacteria. 70% of the bacteria that cause HAIs are resistant to at least one
drug used to treat the condition. Equally important—bacteria within a biofilm are hundreds of
times more resistant to antimicrobials than bacteria without biofilm protection.
DRESSINGS
Dressings are a mainstay of the standard protocol. Dressings can be impregnated with
products to increase or decrease absorption or impregnated with an antimicrobial. Alginate is
an absorbing material derived from seaweed and is found in many dressings. The most recent
technological change in dressing therapy was in 2002 with the introduction of Silver as an
antimicrobial—though recent studies show silver has little to no effect on infection rates or
wound healing.
The fundamentals of wound care have changed very little since the advent of antibiotics in 1939. Why?
Because R&D at healthcare companies has relied on wound care studies that follow the remove-it, kill-it,
and cover-it tradition.
It’s important for investors to be
familiar with Kane Biotech Inc. This
company has developed a product called
DispersinB (DispB) that attempts to disperse
biofilms already formed so that antibiotics
can better attack the bacteria once the
biofilm has been thinned out. The product
does not prevent biofilm formation and
works within a very narrow pH range.
At time of this writing, the FDA has not
approved this product.
NEW: DISPERSION PRODUCTS
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 40 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE PRODUCT
Highlights: Biofilm targeted infection treatment.
The Chelexa BioLexa cream is a topical antibiotic ointment with DTPA that prevents
biofilm formation for a variety of wound care infections. It combines two FDA
approved products:
1. DTPA, which prevents bacteria from forming a defensive matrix of biofilm.
2. A broad-spectrum antibiotic, like Gentamicin, which kills bacteria.
CHELEXA HAS FIVE TYPES OF CUSTOMERS:
CHELEXAB I O S C I E N C E S
WOUND
CLINICS
NURSING
HOMES
HOME CARE NURSE
ASSOCIATIONS
HOSPITALS 	GOVERNMENT
CONTRACTS
Chelexa offers the world’s only biofilm
targeted infection treatment that is
more effective and less expensive
than traditional treatments.
Proprietary Product And
First-Mover Advantage.

Is applicable to over 35 infections, and is effective against 3 types of bacteria
Saves healthcare providers 95% over the course of an 8 week treatment

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE CHELEXA VALUE PROPOSITION
That’s a big market. And with even more growth anticipated in the
future —chronic wounds from diabetes alone are growing at 10%
a year for example —it’s going to get even bigger.
The Chelexa product will lower costs in many ways:
t)PTQJUBMTXJMMOPXIBWFBNVDIDIFBQFSUSFBUNFOUGPSUIF
inevitable hospital acquired infection (HAI).

For large healthcare institutions, wound care clinics, and long

are at best ineffective and at worst further infections:
Chelexa is a wound care company that provides a solution of
two FDA approved products that prevents bacteria from forming
a defensive matrix and then eradicates the infection. Our
product prevents the substance responsible for 80% of human
infectious diseases allowing healthcare providers to lower
overall treatment costs up to 90% for certain HAIs.
THE VALUE OF THE BIOLEXA TREATMENT
The market for wound treatment is approaching $20 billion in costs for healthcare
providers, and $4.6 billion in revenue for product providers.

with the BioLexa cream.
Not Your Everyday Antibiotic Cream
The BioLexa ointment is not just another antibiotic cream. It combines two FDA-
approved ingredients to cure infection. Unlike other treatments, like NPWT, HBOT,
Silver Dressings, which provide costly measures against infection, BioLexa is a more
effective, less expensive treatment that inhibits biofilm. And you can get this in a
tube for only $30.
Chelexa is more effective at treating infection than traditional methods, so
patients suffer less, bear less costs, and heal faster. Hospitals can treat more patients,
get better outcomes, and save money from costly wound complications.

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE SALES CHANNEL
Path to market discussion
The point of entry into the market will be through wound clinics, podiatry offices, and skilled
nursing homes. These places have the highest incidents of acute/chronic wounds.
DIRECT SALES
Texas, California, Ohio, Florida, New York and New Jersey—these Tier 1 territories were chosen
based on the number of nursing homes, wound clinics, home care agencies, long-term care
facilities, lower-extremity amputations and reimbursement rates.
WHOLESALE AGREEMENTS
Obtaining wholesaler distribution agreements with key organizations serving the wound care
market—including Owens and Minor, McKesson, and Cardinal Health—will provide access to
over 10,000 healthcare providers and distribution centers around the United States.
LICENSING AGREEMENTS
Discussions on licensing agreements with large distributors such as Healthpoint Biotherapeutics,
Smith & Nephew, and Covidien PLC (NYSE: COV) have already begun and each has expressed a
strong interest in licensing the product.
CLIENT
www.intersectioncapital.com

should we choose to execute it.
The product will be available in 5 gram and/or
10 gram quantities in both single packs and as
“treatment packs” based on an average chronic
wound course of 14 treatments.
Finished goods will transfer to a fulfillment
warehouse, again in compliance with cGMP
and ISO certifications. This fulfillment house will
break the bulk quantities, pick, pack, and ship to
end-points where patient treatment occurs.

-- 45 of 66 --

CHELEXA	B I O S C I E N C E S
THE TEAM
There are more than 1,000 venture and private equity
firms on the west coast, and they agree on few things
in unison. Perhaps the only single point of agreement is:
teams win.
For healthcare investors looking to manage their
downside risk, they will want to have a CEO and leader
such as Andrew Otterbacher at the helm, making the
important decisions that will affect the scale of their
financial returns. He comes to Chelexa with a blend
of talents that cannot be easily recruited: formal
experience in private equity, entrepreneurial passion
and science know-how. Mr. Otterbacher founded Leah
Ventures Inc as a Private Equity Consulting service and
established NVCI GmbH in Germany with a $5 million
fund in mid-2004 to service Europe and MENA markets
sourcing and evaluating telecommunication deals.
During this time, Mr. Otterbacher was under contract
as a consultant to Dave Mosher, a Managing Director
at Madison Dearborn Partners. Prior to Leah Ventures,
Mr. Otterbacher worked with Monumental Venture
Partners and with Metromedia Fiber Networks as a
Client Security and Network Consultant. Earlier Mr.
Otterbacher was a General Manager for Inform GmbH
– a software company specializing in material flow
optimization, inventory optimization, and Fuzzy-Logic
model development software. Mr. Otterbacher opened
their US office and grew sales to over $3.5 million in 2
years while managing all aspects of the company from
marketing to sales to software installation.
Mr. Otterbacher has a Masters in Engineering
Management from Northwestern University in Evanston,
IL and a Bachelors of Science in Industrial Management
from Purdue University in West Lafayette, IN.
Dr. Herr is currently the Ohio Eminent Scholar at the
University of Cincinnati College of Medicine where
he is an Associate Professor in the Department of
Molecular Genetics, Biochemistry and Microbiology.
Dr. Herr focuses his research in mechanistic studies of
cellular adhesion and receptor-mediated signaling to
include the study of Biofilms. Dr. Herr has over 40 Peer
Reviewed publications and 2 patents to his name.
Dr. Herr holds a PhD in Molecular Biophysics from
Washington University and was a Postdoctoral Fellow
at the California Institute of Technology.
Ms. Potter was president and chief operating officer of
Genentech, Inc. from 2000 to 2005 where she helped
steer the company through one of the most successful
periods in its history. Genentech achieved record
sales and earnings growth during each of the five years
she led the company, launching seven breakthrough
molecules including the blockbuster Avastin.
Prior, Ms. Potter was president of Bristol-Myers
Squibb’s $4 billion, 3,500-person U.S. Cardiovascular/
Metabolics business and launched three new products
including the blockbuster Plavix.
Before working for Bristol-Myers Squibb, Ms. Potter
worked at Merck for fourteen years. During her tenure
at Merck, she established the new pharmaceutical
company, Astra/Merck, Inc. that later merged
with Zeneca to form the company now known as
AstraZeneca.
Ms. Potter currently serves on the board of directors
of Medco Health Solutions, Inc., Everyday Health, Inc.
and 3G BioTech, LLC. Formerly, Ms. Potter served on the
boards of Amazon.com (2004 to 2009) and FoxHollow
Technologies/Ev3 (2006-2007). She also served on
the Dean’s Advisory Board of the Stanford Business
School (2006 to 2010) and on the Citizen’s Financial
Accountability Oversight Committee of the California
Institute for Regenerative Medicine.
Andrew Otterbacher
Chief Executive Officer and
Chairman
Andrew Herr
Chief Medical Officer
Myrtle Potter
Board Member

about the offering. This is not an offer to sell, or a solicitation of an offer to buy land, as such
an offer or solicitation can only come through the offering’s Conservation Memorandum
(“CM”) . This material cannot, and does not, replace the CM, and the CM supersedes this
material in all respects.

-- 49 of 66 --

THE OPPORTUNITY IN 103 WORDS
To save humanity, you have to tackle some really nasty problems. You
already know about them: climate change, clean energy, and species loss.
To us, the problem of species loss stands out. Why?
Because it’s irreversible.
Areas where there is high species concentration have become exceedingly rare and
valuable —out of 192 countries, only 17 make the watch list. None are in Europe. The
U.S. is one. The remaining 15 are in South America, Central America, Africa, and Asia.
All are at risk. But many are difficult to reach. Others are dangerous to operate in
once you reach them. Some are prohibitively expensive to deal with. Several are
targeted by other organizations.
But, there are 4 countries with areas of biodiversity wealth and species
concentration that we have identified as reachable, affordable and saveable. Our
immediate focus is to defend these areas.
This prospectus describes one of our priorities, how we selected it, why it must be
defended, and what it costs to do so.
www.getwsodownload.com

THE PROBLEM: IT’S NOT WHAT YOU THINK
Current gobla data on habitat loss and species extinction.
THE TUMBESIAN ECOSYSTEMS south
america
AREA OF INTEREST: 600,000 ACRES
NATURE
CULTURE &NATURE
There are severe threats to the natural habitats of The Tumbesian Ecosystems that include forest
conversion, the overexploitation of land and pollution sources such as mining, fish processing
plants and petroleum extraction. Other regions of the world are suffering similar fates as seen in
the global data on species that are threatened with extinction.
E

d
a

E

m

a

S
p
e
c
i
e
s
32%
Clearing and logging have reduced some of Peru’s forests to only a few percent of their original
extent. Unless this is your first day in the conservation business, you already know about this.
And because of general awareness, progress is being made:
Protection efforts have started in many of Peru’s high profile habitats. There are 56 conservation
zones in the country, covering almost 13% of the landmass. That’s the good news.
But—some of Peru’s most biodiverse areas are so remote, they aren’t even acknowledged as
habitats that need protection. That’s a serious problem.
In Peru, large and remote areas where logging, overgrazing, and corn fields have replaced 95%
of biodiverse habitats,the lack of management (and community involvement) means that the
process of species extinction is occurring and accelerating.
Nearly everyone who studies conservation—or watches CNN—is already aware that
bulldozers and logging machines are clearing some of the world’s most valuable habitats.
Overgrazing is a severe problem in the remaining natural
areas and sustainable management techniques are
needed in nearly all local communities. Other threats
include the weak government policies for resource
management in this ecosystem, the lack of knowledge
of the bene!its of environmental services and products,
the lack of conservation programs, and the lack of
conservation education for the owners of communal and
private lands.
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 53 of 66 --

NATURE
CULTURE &NATURE
THE SOLUTION: DO MORE FASTER AND CHEAPER NATURE 	CULTURE
$2 per acre
THE 17 MOST BIODIVERSE COUNTRIES (2011)
WE’D LOVE TO TELL YOU THAT OUR EFFORTS WILL SAVE THE SNOW LEOPARD, POLAR BEAR
AND EVEN THE BLUEFIN TUNA. But lets get real. That might cost billions of dollars which is
above our pay grade. We’re field scientists who work away from the limelight and we focus
on just one thing: how to use known science, education, and political persuasion to protect
biodiverse habitats from logging and clearing.
For organizations that offer funds to protect the
environment, and who feel there isn’t enough cost
efficiency and results for their conservation dollar,
Nature & Culture’s NW Peru Project is a way to get
maximum bang-for-the-buck.
We prevent species extinction in the most biodiverse
areas in the world for about $2 an acre (If you just
bought the land, it could cost you up to $300 an acre.)
Unlike other conservation organizations—or corporate
green projects which provide expensive awareness
programs—NCI is operational: more than 90% of the
capital we raise puts boots on the ground in areas that
face immediate danger of species extinction.
And its done in a way that is measured, recorded and
reported.
NCI can do this by putting resources and science into the
hands of Peru’s local conservationists and biologists. We
also help policy makers that are already motivated to
protect these areas.
The projects we support are in the rain forests, cloud
forests, dry forests, and estuaries of Latin America, but
they are always initiated through our relationships with
resident conservationists and conducted in concert with
local mayors, politicians, NGOs, and community leaders.
Our strategy unites nature with culture.
We efficiently safeguard many of Latin America’s remaining
ecosystems by providing financial resources and trusted
guidance to a wide array of local conservationists,
biologists, and their communities.
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 54 of 66 --

NATURE
CULTURE &NATURE	THE NATURE & CULTURE MODEL
THE FIELD SCIENTISTS AT NATURE & CULTURE HAVE A JOYLESS TASK: Find
and catalog the most exotic ecosystems in the world—the ones that are
about to be wiped out.
From this list, we are faced with painful choices. We cannot be
everywhere at once, so we have to prioritize:
Our purpose is to protect habitats where species extinction is imminent.
Northwest Peru: The Tumbesian Ecosystems
To prevent deforestation and clearing of this habitat, we have to get the
government of Peru to declare it as a Regional Protected Area.
If you haven’t done this before—the learning curve is steep. But we’ve
been doing it since 1997. Not only do we get it done, but we can do it for
approximately $2 an acre.
IDENTIFY 	DOCUMENT	DOCUMENT 	DOCUMENT	FUND 	DOCUMENT	PROTECT	1 	2 	3 	4
Nature and Culture’s staff and partners have previously protected
5,000,000 acres of biodiverse ecosystems in Latin America.
If we just buy the land without the support of the people who live there, then you
can guess the outcome: the conservation effort will be temporary. There’s only
one business model that we know of which will protect the land for generations.
FIRST, you have to provide a project
binder and it can’t be scientifically
arcane. It has to be accessible to
governmental officials and locals.
r ACHIEVE
r GOVERNMENT
r IMPLEMENTATION
NEXT, you have to build a groundswell
of interest. Beyond the professionals,
The People have to care.
THEN, you have to get the
government to approve your project
and make a Declaration of Protection.
ONLY THEN, can you go to the
subject site and begin conservation
and protection.
To get any government to declare 600,000 acres as
a Regional Protected Area, you have to do some
specific things:
r GOOD FIELD
SCIENCE
SIGN-OFF
CRITICAL MASS
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 55 of 66 --

Epipedobates is dismissed by most because it’s so tiny,
yet it contains a potent toxin that is also a powerful pain
killer—200 times more powerful than morphine. This is a
social animal, living in groups of up to eight individuals.
13% 56 	10%

-- 56 of 66 --

//by BYRON SWIFT//
TECHNICALLY, WHAT IS BIODIVERSITY?
The power of evolution drives the origin of species to produce a a living legacy that is our responsibility to
protect at this historic crossroads. The magnitude of the threat to biodiversity is enormous.
We have taken over and converted most of the productive lands and forests, poisoned or drained innumer-
able wetlands, over-fished the oceans, and altered the atmosphere. One in three amphibians, one in four
mammals, and one in eight birds are currently facing extinction. Some of the habitats that support biodi-
versity are down to their last remnants.
This data should prompt all of us to take seriously the need to study and defend biodiversity. With the cor-
rect action taken now, it is still possible to save most of the diverse forms of life on our planet.
THERE ARE SOME PROBLEMS THAT ARE FIXABLE. THIS IS ONE.
1We’re losing species
irrecoverably
2 	3 4
We are taking the
land that plants and
animals rely on
We take, but we
don’t give back
This is an imbalance and
it’s not sustainable
Since life began on Earth, five major
mass extinctions and several minor
events have led to large and sudden
drops in biodiversity. The Permian–
Triassic extinction event, 251 million
years ago, was the worst; vertebrate
recovery took 30 million years. The
most recent, the Cretaceous–Tertiary
extinction event, occurred 65 million
years ago—this is the one that killed
the dinosaurs.
But since the dinosaurs, species loss
has now accelerated above any prior
rate, and its driven by human activity.
Estimates of species loss are at a rate
1,000 to 10,000 times as fast as is
typical in the fossil record.
The period since the emergence of
humans has displayed an ongoing
biodiversity reduction and an
accompanying loss of genetic
diversity. Named the Holocene
extinction, the reduction is caused
primarily by human impacts,
particularly habitat destruction.
Biodiversity’s impact on human
health is also a major international
issue..
Biodiversity supports ecosystem
services including air quality, climate
(e.g., CO2 sequestration), water
purification, pollination, and erosion
prevention.
Biodiversity gives rise to many
ecosystem services that are often
not readily visible. It plays a part
in regulating the chemistry of our
atmosphere and water supply.
Biodiversity is directly involved in
water purification, recycling nutrients
and providing fertile soils.
Experiments with controlled
environments have shown that
humans cannot easily build
ecosystems to support human needs;
for example insect pollination cannot
be mimicked, and that activity alone
represents tens of billions of dollars
in ecosystem services per year to
humankind.
For these reasons, the United Nations
designated 2010 as the International
Year of Biodiversity
The term pretty much defines itself, and you don’t need a biology degree to “get it”:
Biodiversity is the variation of life forms within an ecosystem or habitat. The field
scientists at Nature & Culture believe it is a measure of the health of ecosystems.
EXTINCTION RATES ABOVE EXPECTED
ESTIMATED EXTINCT SPECIES BY 2050
EST. TIME FOR VERTEBRATE RECOVERY
1000X
30%
30
OF ALL KNOWN
MILLION YEARS
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL

www.getwsodownload.com

-- 58 of 66 --

NATURE
CULTURE &NATURE
Nothing is more important to us than building
strong relationships with the people who
live near our programs. And not just strong
relationships, but unshakable ones.
Because as
conservationists we have
protected more than five
million acres of biodiverse
habitat and we know over
time, the boundaries of
each habitat will be tested
by policy makers, industrialists and military
groups.
And since we never build fences or physical
boundaries, it’s only the strength of the human
heart that can protect habitat in the long term.
This brings us to the central idea of our programs
and certainly what must be the most important
lesson of the biodiversity crises: Putting up
fences is a short-term fix that will fail in the
long-term. The key risk to “fencing,” as it is
known, is that the people who live in-and-near
our projects will come to feel restricted from
using their own lands. They will eventually slip
past guards and any type of fencing to exploit
the natural resources.
So if you take away the fences and the guards
and the patrols - your remaining option is to
build social programs
that naturally protect the
environment.
Social programs? Again,
you cannot sell anyone
on the value of biodiverse
habitats - people need to arrive at this
conclusion on their own, especially nationalistic
and hard-headed governments.
What you can do is build relationships with
politicians, regional authorities and local people.
That’s what we do.
Our relationships lead to trust, allowing us
to show people their own habitats through a
conservation lens.
Trusted relationships are NCI’s critical skillset.
This is how we gain the opportunity to
permanently conserve large areas.
THE CRITICAL SKILLSET
There’s no more simple truth in the conservation business: If the locals don’t benefit from
your environmental protection program -now and in the future - then the program won’t
last beyond the current generation.
SCIENCE
CULTURE
NATURE
INVESTMENT
Know the
Community
Assess
Species
Actions for Conservation Success
Map
Region
Deploy
Talent
Ensure
Permanence
Determine
Cost
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 59 of 66 --

SCREENING A POTENTIAL SITE
with NATURE & CULTURE
SIZE OF
POTENTIAL
SITE	1
2
3
4
5
When we first consider a project, we look for a size
that’s in our sweet spot of 100,000 to 1 million acres.
We like to work on big projects and important projects—
but we don’t want to overreach.
Next, we use in-country biologists to assess species
diversity and endemism—the number of plants and
animals that are unique to the region. Endemism is the
most critical element to look for because it is at the
heart of biodiversity.
A trend analysis looks at the imminent dangers that are
moving towards the region. These might be logging,
slash and burn pasturage, biofuel plantations, overgraz-
ing, oil extraction, or mining. We go to where the threats
are imminent.
The only way to protect land for less than $100 an acre
is to work with mayors, governors, and community
leaders in order to set up community reserves,
protected reserves, and zoning regulations. This factors
into our accounting. If we can’t meet our cost targets,
we know the project isn’t right for us.
The time, energy and resources devoted to a project
only make sense if the land can be protected in perpe-
tuity. The overarching goal is to create a legacy for the
people and organisms involved. We look for projects
that will provide a legacy for future generations.
6 We can’t succeed on our own, and we know we need
local conservationists with political talent to match their
biology training. We like to find conservationists who will
eventuallymove into government service—where they
can bring us greater levels of support over time.
b We don’t rely on single sources to determine the priority level of a site.
Here’s how NCI analyzes a living asset prior to investment:
DIVERSITY &
“ENDEMISM”
IMMINENT
THREATS
COST
ANALYSIS
STAYING
POWER
LOCAL
TALENT
SELECTING A SITE: OUR APPROACH NATURE

www.getwsodownload.com

-- 60 of 66 --

ECOSYSTEM DETAIL:
The coastal waters are part of the Peruvian Current Large Marine Ecosystem (LME) which is considered a
Class I, highly productive ecosystem. The Peruvian and Humboldt’s high rates of primary and secondary
productivity support the world’s largest fisheries, accounting for nearly 20% of the world’s fish catch.
Foca Island (1,000 acres)
We have an opportunity to declare the first
marine protected area in Peru at Foca Island. It
is the northernmost island of Peru and is unique
because it includes both
tropical and cold water
marine 	species. 	The
proposed 	conservation
area 	(including 	the
surrounding marine area)
contains 	the 	northernmost
breeding 	population 	of
the 	endangered 	Humboldt 	Penguin 	and
other endemic seabird species. It also has
a small breeding colony of fur seals that are
possible hybrids with Galapagos Fur Seals. Its
underwater diversity includes 54 fish species,
50 invertebrate species and significant fields
of gorgonian corals that are endemic.
San Pedro Mangroves (20,500 acres)
These mangroves provide an important habitat
to migrating birds and the endemic Sechura
Fox. San Pedro is a Birdlife Important Bird
Area, providing critical habitat for the Peruvian
Plantcutter and the Peruvian Tern. This wetland
site is listed under the Ramsar Convention for
Wetland protection, and includes 1,000 acres
of mangroves and 20,500 acres of wetlands
and riparian habitat. The mangroves protect
over 200 fish species, some of commercial
importance, to the Peruvian fishing industry.
Virrila Estuary and Ramon and Ñapique La-
goons (125,000 acres)
This 	Peruvian 	coastal 	site 	harbors 	large
numbers of migratory waterfowl. It is an
important 	breeding
site for the charismatic
Chilean 	Flamingo 	and
the rare Peruvian Tern
is 	also 	found 	here.
Adjacent 	sea-grass
(Caulerpa 	filiformis)
habitat is home to the
Pacific Seahorse (Hippocampus ingens) and is
a feeding site for green sea turtles (Chelonia
mydas agassizzi).
Illescas Peninsula (175,000 acres)
Illescas Peninsula is characterized by rocky
hills, sand dunes, dry forest patches and is a
remnant of the formerly isolated Cordillera de
la Costa; combined with the marine coastal
strand, it exhibits a tremendous abundance
and diversity of life. It is one of the few places
where the endangered giant Andean Condors
feed and reproduce Vand is the only site
where the rare Illescas Gecko is found. NCI is
also proposing that a marine extension to the
peninsula be included in a new protected area
that was originally proposed in the 1990’s.
FOCA ISLAND
1,000 ACRES
ILLESCAS
PENNINSULA
175,000 ACRES
VIRRILA ESTUARY
RAMON&NAPIQUE LAGOONS
125,000 ACRES
SAN PEDRO
MANGROVES
20,500 ACRES
Foca Island
San Pedro Mangroves
and Virrila Estuary
Ramon & Ñapique Lagoons
Illescas Forests
AREA OF DETAIL
Piura	
.
North Western Peru is bounded
on three sides by a triangle and a
large box of bananasa
Coastal Habitats
These areas will protect three types of wetlands - estuaries,
lagoons and mangroves . They are especially important for their
unique endemic species, habitat type, marine diversity and im-
portance for birds. The region’s fresh water rivers flow from the
high Andes through the tropical dry forest to the Pacific Ocean
providing fresh water to the ecosystems in between.
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

estimated
15K
JAGUAR
THE TUMBESIAN ECOSYSTEMS south
america
AREA OF INTEREST: 600,000 ACRES
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 62 of 66 --

ECOSYSTEM DETAIL:
Northwestern Peru is a nexus where five distinct habitat types converge: tropical dry forest, tropical deserts,
wetlands, riparian areas, intertidal-coastal areas and ocean. Here the cold Humboldt Current and warm Panama
ocean currents create a unique, transitional area exhibiting tremendous marine and terrestrial diversity. This di-
verse combination of habitats provides some of the highest rates of biodiversity in a small area.
Angostura (20,000 acres)
This 	area 	expands 	the 	Amotape 	Foothills
National Park and contains rare transitional
habitats between the dry tropical forest and
the humid Equatorial Pacific Forest. There
are significant breeding populations of the
endangered Grey Hawk and new amphibian
species have been found here. The national
park service and NCI have already constructed
a field research station/operations center
here for management and research.
Coto de Caza El Angolo (50,000 acres)
NCI 	is 	currently 	seeking 	a 	management
concession from the Peruvian ministry of the
environment to manage at least 50,000 acres
at the Angolo Hunting Reserve (Coto de Caza)
in an effort to conserve this national protected
area’s tropical dry forests. This area is the
second largest protected dry tropical forest
in Peru and along with the adjacent Cerros de
Amotape National Park, it contains the highest
number of globally threatened bird species
(9) of any reserve in Peru. This unmanaged
area is currently threatened with overgrazing,
woodcutting and mining.
Amotape Foothills—Talara (60,000 acres)
This proposal would protect an additional
60,000 acres of endangered and species-
laden dry tropical forest in the foothills adjacent
to the Cerros de Amotape National Park. It
would also create an area to conserving one
of the largest populations of the endangered
Peruvian Plantcutter (Phytotoma raimondii) in
the arid plains at Talara. Talara encompasses
the largest and most intact area of sparse dry
forest in northwestern Peru—an ecosystem
that has been virtually destroyed. The area is
heavily overgrazed and without management
so conservation effort is badly needed to first
protect and then restore this area.
Salitral (125,000 acres)
Salitral 	is 	the 	northern 	boundary 	of 	the
species-rich tropical dry forest conservation
along the slopes of the western Andean
foothills. 	This 	proposed 	protected 	area
contains 70% of the habitat of the critically
endangered White-winged Guan (Penelope
albipennis) and contains approximately 80%
of Peru’s wild population of this species.
The area has been prioritized as an Area of
Importance for the Conservation of Birds (IBA)
by Birdlife International. Site management will
include building fences at critical points of
access for livestock, building an educational
center focused on the White-Winged Guan, the
Spectacled Bear and their habitat needs and
establishing a local management committee.
Tropical Dry Forests
AREA OF DETAIL
Piura	.
Angostura
Coto de Caza
Amotape
Salitral
The dry tropical forests of this region, together with those of
adjacent Ecuador, are known as the Tumbesian Region of Ende-
mism (135,000 km2) and are one of the worlds highest biodi-
versity priorities due to species endemism and degree of threat.
THE REGION 	TEAM	NCI	THE OPPORTUNITY 	THE MODEL
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 63 of 66 --

NW PERU: FIVE DISTINCT HABITATS 	2011
Northwestern Peru is a nexus where five distinct
habitat types converge: Tropical deciduous forests,
mangroves, estuaries, intertidal zones, and marine
ecosystem.
Gray and Gold Warbler: one of the
endemics of the Tumbesian coastal
dry forest ecosystem in NW Peru.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 64 of 66 --

OUR TEAM NATURE
CULTURE &NATURE
WE KNOW LATIN AMERICA. WE’VE INVESTED, ACQUIRED, MANAGED AND PROTECTED 5,000,000 acres of
biodiverse habitat in four countries: Peru, Ecuador, Columbia, and Mexico.
Byron Swift, President
Byron has worked with local partners
throughout Latin America for over two
decades to improve land and resource
conservation and environmental policy. An
environmental lawyer, Byron is an expert in
private lands conservation and has published
widely on biodiversity, industrial pollution and
climate change. He has directed the energy
program at the Environmental Law Institute
(1994-2004) and the US office of IUCN -
World Conservation Union (1998-2004), and
has served as a private foundation officer.
Ivan Gayler, Chair
Ivan is the founding President and Chairman
of the Board of Nature and Culture Interna-
tional. Since 1997, NCI has lead and supported
efforts to establish millions acres of tropical
forest reserves in Ecuador, Peru, Columbia, and
Mexico; including the creation of a 2.5 million
acre UNESCO recognized Biosphere Reserve in
the tropical Andes, 4.5 million acres of Regional
Reserves in the Amazon rainforests, and over
100,000 acres of tropical dry forest including
20,000 acres in Sonora, Mexico.
Ivan has served as President and Board Mem-
ber of the San Diego Museum of Man, a Board
Member of the La Jolla Playhouse, and a
Member of the Director’s Cabinet of Scripps
Institution of Oceanography. Ivan believes our
generation is the last with the opportunity to
save the variety of life on earth.
Luis Alban, Director of Nature & Culture-Peru
Luis initiated our Peru office in 2003. He is a
Biologist (University Ricardo Palma of Lima),
with twelve years experience in projects
dealing with conservation and development,
especially in relation to forest genetic re-
sources. Luis served as scientific director for
reforestation at the University of Piura (1994-
2004), President of the Regional Environ-
mental Commission of Piura (2002-2004),
and Coordinator of the Piura working group
on paramos (2001-2004).
www.getwsodownload.com

www.getwsodownload.com
